evals.prompt: Should we build a brand community? We're a $6M skincare brand with 8% promoter score.
evals.expected_output: Surfaces that 8% promoters is below the threshold where community ROI is structural. Recommends investing in promoter generation first (product improvements, post-purchase experience, NPS detractor handling) before launching a community. Conditions community investment on growing the promoter base. Pulls NPS distribution and LTV to size the deferred case.
assertions: Identifies 8% promoters as below the structural threshold
Pulls modern.surveys.nps_distribution to size the cohort
Recommends promoter-generation work first
Conditions community investment on promoter growth
Names the specific upstream work
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, single next move

evals.prompt: Discord, Slack, Facebook, or Circle for our running-shoe community?
evals.expected_output: Walks platform selection. For a fitness audience Discord is a strong fit (real-time chat, fitness culture present). Conditions on the demographic. Notes Facebook Group as the default-reach option but algorithm-dependent. Circle as the higher-cost hosted option if the brand wants tier control. Recommends Discord for engagement and Circle if tier and education content are central.
assertions: Walks the platform selection framework
Names Discord as fit for fitness audience
Conditions on the demographic profile
Notes Facebook Group algorithm dependency
Names Circle for hosted platform with tier control
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, single next move

evals.prompt: We have 5,000 community members but the community feels dead. What's wrong?
evals.expected_output: Walks the metrics-that-lie framework. Total member count is misleading; active member ratio is the honest metric. Recommends measuring posts-and-comments-in-last-30-days divided by total members. Below 5% is decay. Walks possible causes: weak founder presence, missing tier structure, platform mismatch, unclear purpose. Asks for the active ratio before prescribing the fix.
assertions: Walks the engagement-metrics-that-lie framework
Names active member ratio as the honest metric
Names below 5% as the decay threshold
Asks for active ratio before prescribing
Names possible causes: founder presence, tiers, platform, purpose
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, single next move

evals.prompt: What benefits should our VIP tier offer?
evals.expected_output: Walks the tier structure framework. Tier benefits must escalate meaningfully — role color alone produces no behavior change. Recommends: early product access, founder access, dedicated channels, branded swag, gated drops. Conditions on tier purpose and progression cadence. Notes the annual refresh discipline. Distinguishes the verified-customer tier from the active-contributor tier.
assertions: Walks the tier structure framework
Names the meaningful-escalation rule
Names specific benefits per tier
Recommends annual tier refresh
Distinguishes contributor tier from customer tier
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, single next move

evals.prompt: Our founder doesn't want to be in the community personally. Can we still build one?
evals.expected_output: Possible but harder. Walks the founder presence framework. Communities with active founder presence retain at 2-3x the rate. Without founder, the brand has to compensate with a strong community manager and named brand voice. Recommends conditioning the program scope on what the founder will actually commit to; do not build a founder-AMA program if the founder will not show up. References the cost of staff-only community programs as higher attrition and slower growth.
assertions: Walks the founder presence framework
Names 2-3x retention lift with founder presence
Recommends scoping program to founder commitment
Warns against building features the founder will not staff
Notes higher attrition risk without founder presence
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, single next move

evals.prompt: What's the single most important community metric we should track?
evals.expected_output: Repeat rate delta of members vs matched non-members, or member-attributed revenue if attribution is available. Walks why total member count and DAU mislead. Notes that the metric must be tied to a business outcome (retention or revenue), not to platform activity. Recommends three honest metrics per quarter rather than one to avoid Goodhart's law.
assertions: Names repeat rate delta or member-attributed revenue as the right metric
Walks why total members and DAU mislead
Pulls modern.retention.repeat_rate for the comparison
Recommends three metrics per quarter not one
Names Goodhart-style risk
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, single next move

evals.prompt: Should our community focus on UGC or on customer support?
evals.expected_output: Walks the four purposes framework. Recommends picking one primary purpose. UGC = advocacy purpose, fit for visual or emotional categories with strong word-of-mouth potential. Support = self-help purpose, fit for technical or complex-use categories. Pulls CX volume and NPS distribution to identify which leverage applies. Notes that trying to be both produces a community that does neither well.
assertions: Walks the four community purposes framework
Distinguishes advocacy purpose from support purpose
Recommends picking one primary purpose
Conditions choice on CX volume and category profile
Notes the all-of-the-above failure mode
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, single next move
